content.problemStatement:
Given an array AAA of size NNN, construct an array BBB of size NNN such that :


Every element of BBB is present **at least once** in AAA;
For all 1≤i≤N1\le i \le N1≤i≤N, every element in prefix [A1,A2,…,Ai][A_1,A_2, \ldots, A_i][A1​,A2​,…,Ai​] is present **at least once** in prefix [B1,B2,…,Bi][B_1,B_2,\ldots, B_i][B1​,B2​,…,Bi​].
If multiple such arrays BBB exist, find the **lexicographically largest** BBB.

Note that for two arrays XXX and YYY, each of size NNN, where iii is the smallest index at which they differ, array XXX is said to be lexicographically larger than array YYY when Xi>YiX_i \gt Y_iXi​>Yi​.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of elements in the array.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots ,A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — the elements of the array.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, lexicographically largest array BBB satisfying the conditions.


It can be guaranteed that one such array BBB always exists.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 5·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N space‑separated integers representing the lexicographically largest array B that satisfies the conditions.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case we place a value the first time it appears in A; later positions can use the global maximum. This yields the lexicographically largest valid B.
maxA = 3. New values 1,2,3 must be placed at their first occurrence. Remaining positions use 3.
Only one distinct value exists, so B is identical to A.

solution.approach:
Traverse the array from left to right. Keep a set of values that have already appeared in B. Let maxA be the maximum element of the whole array. For each position i:
1. If Ai has not been placed in B before, we are forced to put Ai at position i (otherwise the prefix condition would be violated, because Ai becomes a new required element and we have only this position to introduce it). Mark Ai as placed.
2. If Ai has already been placed, we are free to choose any value that occurs in A. To obtain the lexicographically largest B we choose the largest possible value, i.e., maxA.
The algorithm is O(N) per test case and uses O(U) extra memory where U is the number of distinct values (≤ N).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, overall O(ΣN) ≤ 5·10^5
solution.spaceComplexity: O(U) for the visited set, ≤ O(N)

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        A = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        maxA = max(A)
        placed = set()
        B = []
        for x in A:
            if x not in placed:
                B.append(x)
                placed.add(x)
            else:
                B.append(maxA)
        out_lines.append(' '.join(map(str, B)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
